content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento. CA 95Rl4-2329 
Patrick Enright, City Attorney 
City of South Lake Tahoe 
1901 Airport Road, Suite 300 
South Lake Tahoe, CA 96150 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 32:!-0886 
December 12, 2012 
Re: 
Your Request for Informal Assistance 
Our File No. 1-12-167 
Dear Mr. Enright: 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest 
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the .. Act").1 Our advice is based solely on the facts 
presented as the Fair Political Practices Commission ( .. Commission") does not act as a finder of 
fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Because your questions are 
general in nature and not limited to a specific governmental decision, we are treating your 
request as one for informal assistance.· 
Additionally, we base our advice solely on the provisions of the Act and do not address 
the applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws, such as common law conflict of 
interest, Government Code Section 1090 or your agency's incompatible activities statement. 
Finally, please note that the Commission does not provide advice relating to past conduct. 
(Regulations 18329(b)(8)(A) and (c)(4)(A).) Therefore, any conclusions contained herein apply 
only to prospective actions. 
QUESTION 
Does the Act prevent Councilmember-elect JoAnn Conner from using her business, 
Golden Bear Events, to put on events such as a 4th of July Parade or a Home and Garden Show in 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2. Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. unless otherwise indicated. 
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114. Regulation 18329(c)(3). copy enclosed.) 


File No. 1-12-167 
Page No. 2 
the City of South Lake Tahoe where she will be required to apply for temporary activity permits 
from the City's Planning Department? 
CONCLUSION 
No. There are no governmental decisions with respect to the events set to come before 
the City Council. In addition, by applying for the permits in her private capacity as owner of 
Golden Bear Events, and not under her authority as a councilmember, Councilmember-elect 
Conner would not be making or participating in making governmental decisions. Moreover, she 
would not be attempting to influence the outcome of a governmental decision because an 
exception exists allowing an official to appear in the same manner as any other member of the 
general public before an agency in the course of its prescribed governmental function solely to 
represent himself or herself on a matter which is related to his or her personal interests. 
FACTS3 
You have requested this advice on behalf of JoAnn Conner who was just elected to the 
South Lake Tahoe City Council.4 Councilmember-elect Conner is the sole owner of Golden 
Bear Events, which promotes and sponsors various events in the City of South Lake Tahoe. 
In July 2012, Golden Bear Events put on a 4th of July Parade that was held on a state 
highway in South Lake Tahoe. Councilmember-elect Conner has expressed interest in holding 
the event again in 2013. In order to put on the parade, Golden Bear Events will be required to 
secure an encroachment permit from Caltrans and a temporary activity permit from the City's 
Planning Department. In order to obtain a temporary activity permit, a person must fill out a 
form which requests specified information about the event and then pay a $40.00 application fee. 
The South Lake Tahoe City Council is not required to make any decisions with respect to the 
issuance of this permit or any other aspects of the parade. 
Golden Bear Events also puts on special events such as a Home and Garden Show. In 
April 2013, it will hold this event at the Recreation Center owned by the City. Prior to her 
election, Councilmember-elect Conner entered into an Agreement5 with the City for such things 
as the hourly/daily rate charged to rent the space. The City Council will not make any decisions 
with respect to the permit required for this event or any other special event. 
3 The facts are drawn from your letter dated November I 5, 2012, Councilmember-elecl Connor's email 
dated November 30, 2012, and a follow-up telephone conversation with her on December 6, 2012. 
4 Your letter stales lhal Councilmember-elect Conner is expected lo be seated as a Councilmember 
following certification of the election results on December 11. 2012. 
5 When an event is held on its property, the City enters into a Temporary License Agreement for the 
duration of the temporary activity permit. The terms and conditions of the Agreement were set by policies and 
procedures established by the City Council prior to Councilmember-elect Conner's election. 


ANALYSIS 
File No. 1-12-167 
Page No. 3 
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials "perform their duties 
in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial 
interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 8100l(b).) Specifically, Section 87100 
prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her 
official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest. 
A public official has a financial interest in a governmental decision, within the meaning 
of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on 
one or more of the public official's economic interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) 
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an individual 
has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision. 
Step One: Is Councilmember-elect Conner a Public Official? 
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials." (Sections 
87100 & 87103; Regulation 18700(b)(l).) Section 82048 defines a public official as "every 
member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency." 
Councilmember-elect Conner will be a public official when her term begins on the South Lake 
Tahoe City Council. 
Step Two: Will Councilmember-elect Conner be making, participating in making or 
influencing a governmental decision? 
A conflict of interest may exist only if an official will make, participate in making, or 
influence a governmental decision. (Section 87100; Regulation 18700(b)(2).) The Commission 
has adopted a series of regulations defining "making," "participating in making," and 
"influencing" a governmental decision. (Regulations 18702-18702.3.) 
A public official "makes" a governmental decision when the official, acting within the 
authority of his or her office or position: (1) votes on a matter; (2) appoints a person; (3) 
obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action; (4) enters into any contractual 
agreement on behalf of his or her agency; or (5) determines not to act, within the meaning of the 
foregoing, unless the determination not to act occurs because of the official's financial interests. 
(Section 87100; Regulation 18702.l(a)(l)-(5).) 
A public official "participates in making a governmental decision" when, acting within 
the authority of his or her position and without significant substantive or intervening review, the 
official negotiates, advises or makes recommendations to the decision-maker regarding the 
governmental decision. (Section 87100; Regulation 18702.2(a)-(b).) 


File No. 1-12-167 
Page No. 4 
At the outset. based on the facts provided. you indicate that there are no governmental 
decisions set to come before the City Council with respect to the Home and Garden Show6 or the 
4
th of July Parade. As such. Councilmember-elect Conner would not be making or participating 
in making governmental decisions involving those events. You have inquired whether 
Councilmember-elect Conner's involvement in filing an application for a temporary activity 
pennit with the City's Planning Department would constitute making or participating in making 
a governmental decision within the meaning of the Act. By filing an application along with an 
application fee in her private capacity as sole owner of Golden Bear Events, and not acting under 
her authority as a councilmember, Councilmember-elect Conner would not be making or 
participating in making a governmental decision.7 
In addition to the prohibition against making or participating in making a governmental 
decision. the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act also prohibit a public official from 
influencing a governmental decision when the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable 
material financial effect on the official's economic interests. "lnfluencing0 a governmental 
decision, insofar as it is relevant to Councilmember-elect Conner's circumstances, is defined as 
follows: 
"With regard to a governmental decision which is within or before an official's agency or 
an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of his or her agency,8 the 
official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence the decision if. for the 
purpose of influencing the decision, the official contacts, or appears before, or otherwise 
attempts to influence, any member, officer. employee or consultant of the agency. 
Attempts to influence include. but are not limited to, appearances or contacts by the 
official on behalf of a business entity, client. or customer." 
(Regulation 18702.J(a).) 
Even assuming Councilmember-elect Conner's involvement in filing for a temporary 
activity permit were characterized as an attempt to influence the Planning Department's 
governmental decision whether to issue the permit, an exception exists so that, notwithstanding 
Regulation 18702.3(a), an official is not attempting to use his or her official position to influence 
a governmental decision if the official: 
6 Golden Bear Events has already entered into a contract with the City for such things as the hourly/daily 
rate lo rent the space. As noted above, the Commission does not provide advice relating to past conduct. 
(Regulations I 8329(b)(8)(A) and (c)(4)(A).) Also, should the issue of the policies and procedures that are used to 
set the terms and conditions of the City's Temporary License Agreements come before the City Council in the 
future, we advise you lo seek further advice from the Commission. 
7 It is also possible that the Planning Department's evaluation of the permit application is "ministerial, 
secretarial, manual, or clerical" in nature such that the Act's prohibition against the making or participating in 
making a governmental decision would not apply in the first instance. (See Regulation l 8702.4(a)( l ).) 
8 Councilmember-elect Conner indicated in our telephone conversation that she believed the City's 
Planning Department was under the budgetary control of the City Council. 


File No. I-12-167 
Page No. 5 
(1) Appears in the same manner as any other member of the general public before 
an agency in the course of its prescribed governmental function solely to represent 
himself or herself on a matter which is related to his or her personal interests. An 
official's 'personal interests' include, but are not limited to: 
(A) An interest in real property which is wholly owned by the official or members 
of his or her immediate family. 
(B) A business entity wholly owned by the official or members of his or her 
immediate family. 
(C) A business entity over which the official exercises sole direction and control, 
or over which the official and his or her spouse jointly exercise sole direction and control. 
(Regulation 18702.4(b )( 1 )(A)-(C).) 
Under the facts provided, Councilmember-elect Conner's involvement with the City's 
Planning Department will be in her private capacity as a member of the general public to 
represent her personal interests in a business entity that she wholly owns. Accordingly, she 
would not be improperly attempting to influence a governmental decision by personally applying 
for a permit with the Planning Department. 
Because we conclude that Councilmember-elect Conner's involvement in applying for a 
temporary activity permit with the Planning Department in her private capacity would not be 
characterized as making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision, we 
end our analysis here. We advise you to seek further advice once you have more information 
regarding specific decisions that are set to come before the City Council so that we may provide 
you with more specific advice at that time. 
JW:jgl 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
Sincerely, 
Zackery P. Morazzini 
General Counsel
